item.title: ICE Reverses Plan to Use Mega-Warehouses for Migrant Detention
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security is backing away from a controversial plan to turn empty warehouses into immigration detention centers and will keep relying largely on existing jails run by private contractors and state and local partners.

item.title: Dog Recovers From Screwworm But Origin Puzzles Officials
item.description: A New Mexico dog infected with the New World screwworm has recovered, but questions remain over how the animal contracted the parasite.

item.title: Even Madonna Has to Fight for Every Scrap of Internet Attention
item.description: Even if you’re one of the biggest pop stars in history, Grindr and Bilt can help you sell albums

item.title: Kenya to Raise $763 Million After Lawmakers Tone Down Tax Plan
item.description: Kenyan lawmakers watered down a slew of tax proposals fronted by the Treasury that would have raised nearly $1 billion for the East African nation’s budget.

item.title: Bank of Canada Can Let Loonie Weaken and Skip Hikes, BofA Says
item.description: The Bank of Canada is likely to keep rates on hold through most of 2027 and allow the Canadian dollar to weaken further even if US monetary policy tightens, Bank of America says.

item.title: Bessent Urged by US Senators to Push China on Undervalued Yuan
item.description: A bipartisan duo of senators has called on Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to step up cooperation with the Group of Seven to pressure China into addressing what they called a deliberate suppression of its exchange rate for commercial advantage.

item.title: Long-End Yields Will End 2026 at Levels Rarely Seen in Last Decade, Survey Shows
item.description: Thirty-year Treasury yields will most likely push back over 5% by the end of the year, the latest Markets Pulse survey showed, signaling some doubts about whether the Federal Reserve will move quickly to enough rein in the recent inflation surge.

item.title: Google’s Secret Warrant Fight Over DOJ Pipe Bomb Probe Revealed
item.description: Google waged a secret court fight against a US warrant demanding identities of hundreds of internet users who searched for the Democratic and Republican party headquarters in Washington before pipe bombs were planted there on the eve of the Capitol riot on Jan. 6, 2021, newly unsealed records reveal.

item.title: US Bond Futures Surge to Drive Wagers on a July Fed Rate Hike
item.description: The Treasury market saw a record surge in futures trading that underpins bets that the Federal Reserve’s next move will be to boost interest rates.

item.title: USMCA Talks Take Virtual Turn as Trump Fuels More Doubts on Deal
item.description: The next scheduled negotiating session over the future of the North American free trade pact will take place virtually at the start of next month, according to the Mexican minister leading the talks, who has sought to project optimism for a deal even as US President Donald Trump has adopted the opposite stance in recent days.

item.title: Nigeria Tax Revenue Jumps 49% on Overhaul, New Oil Levies
item.description: Nigeria’s revenue surged 49% in the first five months of the year, exceeding government projections as an overhaul of the tax system and new levies on industries including petroleum and mining boosted collections.

item.title: Tesla Stock Underperforms SpaceX in First Week After IPO
item.description: Tesla Inc. stock is set to end the holiday-shortened week lower after SpaceX went public last Friday.

item.title: Bitcoin Falls Back Toward $60,000 on Strategy Shares, Rate Fears
item.description: Bitcoin extended its slide back toward the $60,000 level. The decline is driven by mounting concerns over the unraveling of Strategy Inc.’s funding mechanism, alongside rate-hike fears that are dampening demand for riskier assets.

item.title: Brazil Assets Slide as Central Bank Rate Cut Spurs Market Angst
item.description: Brazilian markets sold off on Thursday as investors criticized the central bank for cutting interest rates while flagging a worsening inflation outlook.

item.title: BMO-led $775 Million Pellera Loan Deal Comes at Steep Discount
item.description: Investors are demanding steep discounts to buy debt banks have been stuck holding since financing a leveraged buyout tied to Pellera Technologies, an HIG Capital-backed software company, for over a year.

item.title: Jeremy Grantham Points to a Watershed Moment for Big Tech
item.description: Everyone’s battling on the same frontier.

item.title: Record $21 Billion Water Bonds Sold With Trump Budget Cuts Ahead
item.description: Water utilities are selling bonds at a record pace to upgrade aging pipes and meet tougher regulations as they prepare for a potential pullback in federal funding.

item.title: Square Enix Takes on ‘Zelda’ With ‘The Adventures of Elliot’
item.description: The new game is Square Enix’s first attempt to blend action combat with an ‘HD-2D’ art style

item.title: A Return to $3 Gasoline? Here’s What It Will Take
item.description: Oil prices have plummeted following news of a US-Iran deal that is meant to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, but US drivers probably will have to wait a while for cheaper gasoline. Traders and analysts say that prices are unlikely to fall to pre-crisis levels until stockpiles of crude oil and gasoline are replenished, which probably won’t happen before the end of 2026.

item.title: Biotech Kardigan Rises Nearly 2% After $400 Million IPO
item.description: Kardigan Inc. shares edged up nearly 2% in its trading debut, after the heart-health focused biotech raised $400 million in an initial public offering.

item.title: Paramount-Warner’s ‘Too Low’ Deal Odds Leave Room for Arb Profit
item.description: Paramount Skydance Corp.’s proposed takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. just won crucial US approval — but with many hurdles remaining, traders still see opportunity to profit from how this $110 billion transaction will play out.

item.title: Amazon Workers Say Data Center Testimony Prompted Interrogation
item.description: Three Amazon.com Inc. workers say the company violated a Seattle ordinance by interrogating them after they testified in favor of regulation of data centers in the city.

item.title: Bank of England Looks to Maradona Theory to Avoid Painful Hikes
item.description: Faced with ongoing volatility in the oil market, Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey and his colleagues are trying to strike a path that’s tough on inflation without harming growth and jobs. To do so, they’re taking a history lesson in football.

item.title: First Brands Lender Can’t Distribute Cash From Bankruptcy Sales
item.description: Aequum Capital Financial II is temporarily barred from distributing any cash it collects from the inventory sales taking place as part of bankrupt auto-parts supplier First Brands’ liquidation, a judge ruled Wednesday.

item.title: Pimco Made $2 Billion Bet on Colombia Ahead of First Round Vote
item.description: Funds managed by Pacific Investment Management Co. made a $2 billion bet on Colombia’s local government debt last month, ahead of the first round of a presidential election that is likely to reshape the nation’s economic model.

item.title: US Acts to Accelerate Power Grid Hook-Ups for AI Data Centers
item.description: US regulators have taken their biggest step yet to speed the connection of data centers to the country’s grids while simultaneously attempting to slow surging utility bills that have angered Americans.

item.title: Amazon Is in Talks to Sell Nvidia Rival Chips to Other Firms
item.description: Amazon.com Inc. is in talks to sell its custom-made artificial intelligence chips for use in other companies’ data centers, a key expansion of its efforts to cut into Nvidia Corp.’s dominance.

item.title: Massachusetts Court Strikes Down Tax Cut Ballot Measure
item.description: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court struck down a ballot proposal for a state income-tax cut that was backed by certain business groups.

item.title: Apollo’s Shutterfly Debt Concessions Expose AI, Competition Risk
item.description: Shutterfly’s track record in credit markets has been far from picture-perfect, but its latest refinancing laid bare a newer challenge: the cost of managing burdensome debt amid intensifying competition in an artificial intelligence economy.

item.title: Why AI’s All-You-Can-Eat Buffet Is Coming to an End
item.description: Silicon Valley spent much of the past few years feverishly pushing consumers and businesses to inject artificial intelligence into every corner of their lives. Now AI developers want their customers to pay more for the technology they actually use.
